title: Conics & quadrics
summary: Classify conics and quadrics from their two associated symmetric matrices. The small matrix $A_{22}$ (the quadratic part) fixes the type via $\det A_{22}=\lambda_1\lambda_2$ — positive ⟹ elliptic, negative ⟹ hyperbolic, zero ⟹ parabolic — while $\det A_{33}$ separates proper from degenerate conics. Eigenvalue signs give the character of definition and reduce the conic to canonical form $\lambda_1X^2+\lambda_2Y^2=\mu$. The same machinery, one dimension up, classifies ellipsoids, hyperboloids, cones, cylinders and paraboloids.
objectives: Build the symmetric matrix of a quadratic form by halving mixed coefficients, and write a conic/quadric in the form v^T S v
Assemble the matrices A_33 and A_22 (and the 4x4/3x3 versions for quadrics) from a degree-2 equation
Classify a conic as ellipse, hyperbola, or parabola using det A_22 (type) and det A_33 (degenerate vs proper)
Distinguish real from imaginary ellipses and identify degenerate conics (pairs of lines, point, empty set)
Determine the character of definition (positive/negative definite, indefinite) from eigenvalue signs, using Descartes' rule as a shortcut
Reduce a central conic to canonical form lambda1 X^2 + lambda2 Y^2 = mu by diagonalizing A_22, and find the centre by translation
Classify central quadrics (ellipsoid, hyperboloids, cone, cylinder, paraboloid) and decide whether they contain a line or a circle

blocks.text: What is a conic?

A **conic** is the set of points $(x,y)\in\mathbb{R}^2$ satisfying a degree-2 equation
$$Ax^2 + 2Bxy + Cy^2 + 2Dx + 2Ey + F = 0,$$
where $A,B,C$ are not all zero. The coefficients with mixed terms carry a factor $2$ purely for convenience: it makes the associated matrices symmetric with no extra fractions on the cross terms. The familiar shapes — **ellipse**, **hyperbola**, **parabola** — are the non-degenerate conics; but the same equation can also collapse into pairs of lines, a single point, or even the empty set. The whole game of this topic is: read off two matrices, compute a few determinants and eigenvalue signs, and **classify** the conic without ever drawing it.

A **quadric** is the 3-D analogue: the set of $(x,y,z)\in\mathbb{R}^3$ satisfying a degree-2 equation in three variables. Ellipsoids, hyperboloids, cones, paraboloids and cylinders are all quadrics, and they are classified by exactly the same matrix machinery, just one dimension up.
Quadratic forms and the symmetric matrix

A **quadratic form** in $x_1,\dots,x_n$ is a homogeneous degree-2 polynomial $q(v)=\sum_{i,j} a_{ij}x_i x_j$. It can always be written as $q(v)=v^\top S\, v$ with $S$ **symmetric**. Because the coefficient of $x_ix_j$ ($i\neq j$) splits as $a_{ij}+a_{ji}$, we put **half** of each mixed coefficient into each off-diagonal slot, which makes $S$ symmetric.

blocks.title: Building the symmetric matrix
blocks.text:
For $q(x,y,z)=2x^2+y^2+5z^2+6xy+2yz$, the diagonal entries are the pure-square coefficients $2,1,5$. Halve each mixed coefficient: $6xy\Rightarrow 3$ in the $(1,2)$ and $(2,1)$ slots; $2yz\Rightarrow 1$ in the $(2,3)$ and $(3,2)$ slots; there is no $xz$ term, so $0$. Hence
$$S=\begin{pmatrix}2&3&0\\3&1&1\\0&1&5\end{pmatrix},\qquad q(v)=v^\top S\,v.$$

**Do not** put the full mixed coefficient on the off-diagonal. The cross term $4xy$ gives off-diagonal entry $2$, **not** $4$. Forgetting the factor $\tfrac12$ silently changes the eigenvalues and ruins the classification.
The two matrices of a conic

From $Ax^2+2Bxy+Cy^2+2Dx+2Ey+F=0$ we read off two symmetric matrices. The **big** $3\times3$ matrix $A_{33}$ uses ALL coefficients (with halved mixed terms: the $D,E,B$ are the half-coefficients of $2Dx,2Ey,2Bxy$). The **small** $2\times2$ matrix $A_{22}$ is just the top-left block — the quadratic part. Everything about the conic's classification is encoded in $\det A_{33}$, $\det A_{22}$, and the eigenvalue signs of $A_{22}$.

**Two-determinant rule.**
- $\det A_{33}\neq0$ ⟹ the conic is **non-degenerate** (proper); $\det A_{33}=0$ ⟹ **degenerate** (splits into lines/point).
- $\det A_{22}>0$ ⟹ **elliptic** type; $\det A_{22}<0$ ⟹ **hyperbolic** type; $\det A_{22}=0$ ⟹ **parabolic** type.
Classification by eigenvalues of the small matrix

Note that $\det A_{22}=\lambda_1\lambda_2$, the product of the eigenvalues of $A_{22}$. So the type is decided by the **signs** of $\lambda_1,\lambda_2$:
- **Same sign** ($\det A_{22}>0$): elliptic type. Diagonalizing rotates the equation to $\lambda_1 X^2+\lambda_2 Y^2=\mu$. A real ellipse if $\mu$ matches the eigenvalue sign; an **imaginary** (empty) ellipse if it does not.
- **Opposite signs** ($\det A_{22}<0$): hyperbolic type. Gives a hyperbola if non-degenerate, or a pair of intersecting lines if degenerate.
- **One eigenvalue zero** ($\det A_{22}=0$): parabolic type. Gives a parabola if non-degenerate, or parallel/coincident lines if degenerate.

This is the **canonical form** obtained after rotating (to kill the $xy$ term) and, if the conic is central, translating (to kill the linear terms). The coefficients $\lambda_1,\lambda_2$ are exactly the eigenvalues of $A_{22}$; the rotation matrix is built from its orthonormal eigenvectors.

**Refinement for non-degenerate central conics.** Once $\det A_{33}\neq0$ and $\det A_{22}>0$ (elliptic), check the sign of $\operatorname{tr}(A_{22})\cdot\det A_{33}$: if it is **negative** you get a real (proper) ellipse, if **positive** an imaginary ellipse with no real points (e.g. $x^2+2y^2=-1$).
Descartes' rule of signs — a shortcut for eigenvalue signs

You rarely need the actual eigenvalues, only their **signs**. For a $2\times2$ symmetric $A_{22}$, the characteristic polynomial is $\lambda^2-(\operatorname{tr})\lambda+\det$. Count sign changes $v(p)$ in the coefficient list to get the number of positive roots. Even faster: $\det A_{22}<0$ already forces opposite signs (one $+$, one $-$). $\det>0$ with $\operatorname{tr}>0$ ⟹ both positive; $\det>0$ with $\operatorname{tr}<0$ ⟹ both negative.

blocks.title: Sign of eigenvalues via Descartes
blocks.text: For $q=5x^2+4xy+8y^2$, $A_{22}=\begin{pmatrix}5&2\\2&8\end{pmatrix}$, $p(\lambda)=\lambda^2-13\lambda+36$. Coefficient signs $(1,-13,36)$ have $2$ sign changes ⟹ $2$ positive eigenvalues (they are $4$ and $9$). Both positive ⟹ **positive definite**, and $q=1$ is an **ellipse**.
Definite / indefinite quadratic forms

A quadratic form $q$ (equivalently its symmetric matrix) is **positive definite** if $q(v)>0$ for all $v\neq 0$, **negative definite** if $q(v)<0$ for all $v\neq0$, and **indefinite** if it takes both signs. The link to eigenvalues: positive definite ⟺ all eigenvalues $>0$; negative definite ⟺ all eigenvalues $<0$; indefinite ⟺ there is a positive AND a negative eigenvalue.

Elliptic type ⟺ $A_{22}$ is positive or negative **definite**. Hyperbolic type ⟺ $A_{22}$ is **indefinite**. Parabolic type ⟺ $\det A_{22}=0$ (a zero eigenvalue, semi-definite). This is the bridge between the 'definite/indefinite' language and the conic type.
The standard solving method

steps.label: Write the equation in standard form
steps.text: Move everything to one side: $Ax^2+2Bxy+Cy^2+2Dx+2Ey+F=0$. Read off $A,\dots,F$, remembering mixed coefficients are HALVED.

steps.label: Degenerate or not?
steps.text: Compute $\det A_{33}$. If $\neq0$ the conic is proper; if $=0$ it is degenerate (lines/point).

steps.label: Find the type
steps.text: Compute $\det A_{22}=\lambda_1\lambda_2$. Sign $>0$: elliptic; $<0$: hyperbolic; $=0$: parabolic.

steps.label: Resolve real vs imaginary / which degenerate case
steps.text: For ellipses use the $\operatorname{tr}(A_{22})\cdot\det A_{33}$ sign (negative ⟹ real). For degenerate conics, intersect with a test line to see if you get two distinct lines, a double line, or a point.

steps.label: Reduce to canonical form (if asked)
steps.text: Diagonalize $A_{22}$: eigenvalues $\lambda_1,\lambda_2$ become the coefficients in $\lambda_1X^2+\lambda_2Y^2=\mu$; orthonormal eigenvectors give the rotation.

blocks.text: Worked classification

blocks.title: Classify x² + 2y² + 4xy = 1
blocks.text:
Standard form: $x^2+4xy+2y^2-1=0$, so $A=1,\;2B=4\Rightarrow B=2,\;C=2,\;D=E=0,\;F=-1$.
$$A_{33}=\begin{pmatrix}1&2&0\\2&2&0\\0&0&-1\end{pmatrix},\quad A_{22}=\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\2&2\end{pmatrix}.$$
$\det A_{33}=-1\cdot(1\cdot2-2\cdot2)=-1\cdot(-2)=2\neq0$ ⟹ non-degenerate. $\det A_{22}=2-4=-2<0$ ⟹ hyperbolic type. Conclusion: a **proper hyperbola**.
Reduction to canonical form

blocks.title: Reduce x² + 4xy − 2y² = −1
blocks.text: Here $A_{22}=\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\2&-2\end{pmatrix}$, $p(\lambda)=(1-\lambda)(-2-\lambda)-4=\lambda^2+\lambda-6=(\lambda+3)(\lambda-2)$, so $\lambda_1=-3,\lambda_2=2$ — opposite signs ⟹ **hyperbola**. The orthonormal eigenvectors $\tfrac1{\sqrt5}(1,-2)$ and $\tfrac1{\sqrt5}(2,1)$ rotate the equation into $-3X^2+2Y^2=-1$. The canonical coefficients are exactly the eigenvalues of $A_{22}$.
Quadrics in 3-D

A quadric **contains a line** only when it is ruled: cones, hyperboloids of one sheet, hyperbolic paraboloids, and cylinders. An **ellipsoid never contains a line** (it is bounded). A surface **contains a circle** when two of the eigenvalues of $B$ are equal (rotational symmetry) — e.g. $x^2+y^2+3z^2=1$ has circular cross-sections, but $x^2+2y^2+3z^2=1$ does not.

question.prompt: For the conic $x^2+2y^2+4xy=-1$, the matrix $A_{22}=\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\2&2\end{pmatrix}$. What is its type?

options.content: Parabolic, because there is an $xy$ term
Cannot be determined without the linear terms

question.explanation: The type is fixed by $\det A_{22}=\lambda_1\lambda_2$. Here $\det A_{22}=1\cdot2-2\cdot2=-2<0$, so the eigenvalues have opposite signs and the conic is of hyperbolic type. A) confuses a single entry with the determinant; C) an $xy$ term only signals a rotation is needed, not parabolic type (parabolic requires $\det A_{22}=0$); D) the type depends only on the quadratic part $A_{22}$, not on $D,E$.

question.prompt: The conic $4x^2-y^2=0$ has $\det A_{33}=0$ and $\det A_{22}=-4<0$. What is it?

options.content: A degenerate hyperbola: a pair of intersecting lines

question.explanation: $\det A_{22}<0$ gives hyperbolic type, but $\det A_{33}=0$ makes it degenerate. A degenerate hyperbolic conic is a pair of lines crossing at one point: indeed $4x^2-y^2=(2x-y)(2x+y)=0$ gives $y=\pm2x$. A) requires $\det A_{33}\neq0$; B) needs $\det A_{22}>0$; D) the empty set arises only for imaginary ellipses.
question.theory: $\det A_{22}<0$ and $\det A_{33}=0$ ⟹ two intersecting lines.
